rationale.R1.1: Improving the draft centrally includes sharpening the thesis, contribution framing, and relation between verbal-confidence elicitation and calibration under shift.
rationale.R1.2: The draft's argument chain from setup to results to interpretation would be expected to be checked for coherence as part of improvement.
rationale.R1.3: Several numerical claims depend on reported tables and benchmark results, so aligning claims with the stated evidence is a central responsibility.
rationale.R1.5: The excerpt mixes experimental setup, results, related work, and ablation discussion, making section-level narrative organization centrally relevant.
rationale.R1.6: The open-ended request to improve a draft most directly makes sentence- and paragraph-level clarity, flow, and concision load-bearing.
rationale.RX.1: Any improvement projection must preserve uncertainty about unstated tables, appendices, and prior-work details not visible in the artifact.
rationale.RX.2: The draft contains strong claims about distribution shift and rationale benefits, so avoiding unsupported overclaiming is always active.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within improving the draft rather than performing new experiments, rewriting the artifact here, or auditing beyond the request.
rationale.RX.4: Calibration claims can affect downstream interpretation of model reliability, so harm from overstated robustness or confidence must be avoided.
rationale.RX.5: Changes or judgments would need to remain traceable to the provided artifact, reported metrics, and cited works.
